en.extract:
The Sangihe cicadabird (Edolisoma salvadorii) is a passerine bird in the family Campephagidae that is endemic to the island of Sangir, also written as "Sangihe", and the Talaud Islands. These islands lie northeast of Sulawesi in Indonesia. The species was formerly considered to be conspecific with the Sulawesi cicadabird.


Taxonomy
The Sangihe cicadabird was formally described in 1878 by the English ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe based on specimens collected by Adolf Bernhard Meyer on the island of Sangir, also spelled "Sangihe", which lies north of Sulawesi in Indonesia. Sharpe coined the binomial name Edoliisoma salvadorii where the specific epithet was chosen to honour the Italian ornithologist Tommaso Salvadori. The Sangihe cicadabird was formerly considered to be conspecific with the Sulawesi cicadabird (Edolisoma morio). It was elevated to species status based on the difference in the female plumage and the genetic divergence.
Two subspecies are recognised:

E. s. salvadorii Sharpe, 1878 – Sangihe Islands (northeast of Sulawesi)
E. s. talautense Meyer, AB & Wiglesworth, 1895 – Talaud Islands (northeast of Sulawesi)


References

The '''Sangihe cicadabird''' ('''''Edolisoma salvadorii''''') is a [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Campephagidae]] that is endemic to the island of [[Sangir Island|Sangir]], also written as "Sangihe", and the [[Talaud Islands Regency|Talaud Islands]]. These islands lie northeast of [[Sulawesi]] in Indonesia. The species was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[Sulawesi cicadabird]].

==Taxonomy==
The Sangihe cicadabird was [[species description|formally described]] in 1878 by the English ornithologist [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] based on specimens collected by [[Adolf Bernhard Meyer]] on the island of [[Sangir Island|Sangir]], also spelled "Sangihe", which lies north of [[Sulawesi]] in Indonesia. Sharpe coined the [[binomial name]] ''Edoliisoma salvadorii'' where the specific epithet was chosen to honour the Italian ornithologist [[Tommaso Salvadori]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Sharpe | first1=R. Bowdler | author1-link=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | date=1878 | title=On the collections of birds made by Dr. Meyer during his expedition to New Guinea and some neighbouring islands | journal=Mittheilungen aus dem K. Zoologischen Museum zu Dresden | volume=3 | pages=349–372 [367] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/31377380 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=187 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480862 }}</ref> The Sangihe cicadabird was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[Sulawesi cicadabird]] (''Edolisoma morio''). It was elevated to species status based on the difference in the female plumage and the [[genetic divergence]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Bristlehead, butcherbirds, woodswallows, Mottled Berryhunter, ioras, cuckooshrikes | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=22 September 2024 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Pedersen | first1=M.P. | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Joseph | first3=L. | last4=Rahbek | first4=C. | last5=Jønsson | first5=K.A. | date=2018 | title=Phylogeography of a 'great speciator' (Aves: ''Edolisoma tenuirostre'') reveals complex dispersal and diversification dynamics across the Indo-Pacific | journal=Journal of Biogeography | volume=45 | issue=4 | pages=826–837 | doi=10.1111/jbi.13182| bibcode=2018JBiog..45..826P | hdl=11250/2593769 | hdl-access=free }}</ref>

Two subspecies are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''E. s. salvadorii'' [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1878 – [[Sangihe Islands]] (northeast of [[Sulawesi]])
* ''E. s. talautense'' [[Adolf Bernhard Meyer|Meyer, AB]] & [[Lionel William Wiglesworth|Wiglesworth]], 1895 – [[Talaud Islands]] (northeast of [[Sulawesi]])
